intro: An inventory of the AI-platform layer built into the dk-microservices monorepo — the agents, LLM pipelines, rules, guardrails, knowledge base and tooling that let Cursor work as a grounded teammate. The platform serves two roles from one monorepo — backend development (Dev) and test automation (TA) — each with its own standards, rules, hooks and knowledge (the Cursor config for each role lives at the repo root and in the NexusApiRegressionTests module respectively). Work is grouped below by capability.

categories.title: Dual-Role AI Platform (Backend + TA)
categories.role: One monorepo, two operating models. Each task is classified as backend development or test automation, and each role gets its own rules, commands, hooks and knowledge — so an agent behaves like the right specialist and one role's constraints never leak into the other.

items.name: Ticket-first role routing
items.summary: An always-on baseline rule makes every agent classify the work as DEV (product behaviour) vs TA (regression tests) before acting — the “MS -” title is explicitly not a signal — so it adopts the correct standards from the first message.

items.name: Nested AGENTS.md briefs
items.summary: A role-agnostic brief at the repo root and a self-contained brief in the TA module — nearest-wins and auto-loaded — orient each session without cross-contaminating the other role.

items.name: Backend (DEV) standard set
items.summary: The developer hat: 12 rules, 10 commands, 3 write/commit hooks and the backend knowledge base govern REST, persistence, messaging and validation work under project/** and gateway/**.

items.name: Test-automation (TA) standard set
items.summary: The TA hat: 7 rules, 16 commands, 7 hooks, a dedicated doc set and this dashboard govern Spock regression authoring inside the NexusApiRegressionTests module.

items.name: Scope isolation
items.summary: Glob-scoped rules plus a scope-guard hook keep each role's rules loaded only on its own paths and stop edits from straying outside the intended module.

categories.title: Autonomous Agents & Orchestration
categories.role: Agentic systems that take actions end-to-end — event-driven and scheduled — with human-in-the-loop gates, not just chat responses.

items.name: Shift-left test auto-authoring
items.summary: A feature reaching “In Review” fires a repository_dispatch that runs a headless cursor-agent: it reads the feature ticket + git diff, writes a test plan, implements Spock specs, runs the review gate, and opens an unmerged PR for a human.

items.name: Auto-merge on QA deploy
items.summary: Dual-gate autonomous merge: the TA PR squash-merges only when the linked feature reaches “In QA” AND a human on the approver allow-list approved the exact head SHA. Never merges unreviewed work.

items.name: Bot PR authorship
items.summary: A dedicated bot identity opens PRs so the maintainer can be the reviewer/approver — sidesteps GitHub's self-review deadlock and keeps the approval gate meaningful.

items.name: Scheduled agent jobs
items.summary: Seven headless `agent -p` jobs: nightly triage, dashboard publish, morning report, weekly digest, coverage refresh, OpenAPI-drift authoring, and the nutshell post — the always-on autonomy loop.

items.name: Cursor Automations (cloud)
items.summary: AUTOMATIONS.md specifies how the headless jobs can run as Cursor Automations on cloud compute (a fixed prompt with granted tools/MCPs, cron triggers, configured in the Cursor Cloud Agent dashboard) — a managed alternative to the local/TeamCity `agent -p` loop.

categories.title: LLM Pipelines & Applications
categories.role: Production LLM features with structured output, deterministic fallbacks and grounding — the applied “LLM app” layer of the platform.

items.name: Hybrid nightly nutshell (root-cause analysis)
items.summary: TeamCity emits an evidence pack; a cursor-agent (claude-opus-4-8-thinking-high) investigates the new failures, classifies the likely cause (infra vs code change vs test/data), attributes code regressions to a commit + developer, and emits a structured nutshell + human report. A deterministic rules engine is the fallback.

items.name: Evidence-pack contract
items.summary: A sanitized, truncated JSON contract (failures, clusters, VCS changes + authors, prior build) purpose-built as the LLM's input — context engineering so the model reasons on facts, not noise.

items.name: Two-surface delivery
items.summary: One nutshell object renders both the dashboard Investigation view (long-form, ordered findings) and the compact Teams Adaptive Card (headline + likely cause + top findings).

items.name: Grounded prompt design
items.summary: The nutshell prompt enforces a strict output schema and safety rails: read real failure messages (not coarse cluster labels), never invent blame, and docs/automation-only changes can't be named as a cause of test failures.

categories.title: Context & Knowledge Engineering
categories.role: The biggest lever on agent quality: curate rules, grounding docs and retrievable knowledge so agents act like senior teammates and don't hallucinate.

items.name: Cursor rules system
items.summary: 19 rules (.mdc) — an always-on baseline plus glob-scoped conventions — encode scope boundaries, REST/persistence/messaging conventions and the ticket-first workflow so behaviour is correct from the first message.

items.name: Backend (DEV) knowledge base
items.summary: 27 curated grounding docs — architecture, domain model, data schema, service catalog and dependencies, PES2 events, config toggles, plus 5 ADRs — hand-written so agents reason on repo facts instead of guessing.

items.name: TA knowledge base
items.summary: 24 test-automation grounding & workflow docs — authoring guide, suite tiers, flaky/retry policy, contract testing, CI pipeline, reporting and observability runbooks (Honeycomb, CloudWatch) — so TA agents ground on this module's own conventions, not the backend's.

items.name: Mined review rulebook
items.summary: Coding conventions and a review checklist distilled from real PR-review threads (with provenance) capture the unwritten rules a senior reviewer would apply, so agents produce review-ready code.

items.name: Known-unknowns ledger
items.summary: A living KNOWLEDGE_GAPS doc records what the platform does not yet know, so agents cite where an answer lives or say they can't confirm it rather than inventing one.

items.name: Slash-command library
items.summary: 26 repeatable workflows (onboard service, implement ticket, add endpoint, triage, author-from-OpenAPI, review gate, PR description…) that turn tribal know-how into invokable playbooks for humans and agents.

items.name: Golden-PR convergence (teach-from-pr)
items.summary: An eval loop that replays already-merged “golden” tickets: blind agents re-solve them at the pre-PR state, get graded on tests/conventions/similarity, and the rules, docs and hooks — never the prompt — are improved until the weakest model passes.

categories.title: Guardrails, Safety & Evaluation
categories.role: Make autonomy safe and measurable — deterministic guards, review gates and standards so agents can't drift, leak secrets, or weaken tests.

items.name: Deterministic hooks
items.summary: 10 write-time / shell-time guards: scope boundary, template-preflight, test-authoring preflight, comment-guard, coding-rule lint, and shell guards that block force-push and gate PR creation — enforced on interactive and headless runs alike.

items.name: Pre-PR review gate
items.summary: A command that launches Bugbot + security-review subagents on the diff and resolves findings before a PR is marked ready — an automated second pair of eyes.

items.name: Copilot Auto-Review standard
items.summary: The authoritative 20-section coding standard that drives GitHub's PR review bot; the Cursor rules parallel and extend it for agent sessions.

items.name: Platform-ask process
items.summary: When a fix belongs in production or another team's tree, the agent files a ticket-style “platform ask” instead of editing out of scope — keeping autonomous work inside safe boundaries.

items.name: Autonomy ceilings
items.summary: Hard, enforced limits baked into hooks + workflows: never merge your own PR, never force-push master, never weaken an assertion to make a real bug pass.

categories.title: Tooling & MCP Integration
categories.role: The platform plumbing — connect agents to the org's tools and data through MCP and CLIs so they act with real context.

items.name: MCP server integration
items.summary: Wired Atlassian (Jira/Confluence), GitHub, Honeycomb, Lucid and Playwright MCP servers with templated config and headless auth patterns for CI agents; where no MCP is live (TeamCity is admin-gated) agents fall back to REST APIs and CLIs (`gh`, TeamCity REST, CloudWatch).

items.name: Detached-agent CLIs
items.summary: Helper CLIs (TeamCity REST, JUnit fetch, CloudWatch logs) that give headless/CI agents tool access where an MCP isn't available yet.

items.name: Browser MCP automation
items.summary: Drive web UIs an API can't reach — Jira Automation rules, GitHub settings, TeamCity config — from inside an agent session using the Playwright MCP (and Cursor's built-in browser).

categories.title: Observability & Evidence
categories.role: Feed runtime truth to agents and humans — evidence-based decisions instead of guesses; observe both the system and the agents.

items.name: Honeycomb-informed triage
items.summary: Correlate failing tests with the QA dataset in Honeycomb (via MCP, read-only) to separate product bugs from test/data bugs from infra flakes, and root-cause flakes through traces.

items.name: CloudWatch log root-cause analysis
items.summary: Log-native incident investigation over prod/QA application logs via AWS SSO + Logs Insights — origin exception, stack trace, downstream cascade.

items.name: End-of-turn agent digest
items.summary: A stop hook regenerates a machine- and human-readable test digest at the end of a run and nudges for the mandatory “files changed” summary — lightweight observability over the agents themselves.

items.name: Evidence-driven decisions
items.summary: The nutshell turns raw failures + VCS changes into an attributed root cause; the dashboard surfaces TeamCity-native, agent-readable reporting instead of opaque pass/fail.

categories.title: AI Knowledge Extraction
categories.role: Use AI to mine a large brownfield codebase into durable, trustworthy documentation — shrinking tribal knowledge at scale.

items.name: AI-DLC reverse-engineering
items.summary: A phased reverse-engineering skill that analyzes a service and produces one consolidated README (business overview, architecture, API surface, dependencies, data stores, tech debt, security findings).

items.name: 46 generated service READMEs
items.summary: 46 of ~114 services documented, each with an “Analyzed” provenance banner and “Needs Human Verification” flags, tracked in a status ledger with a spot-check legend.

items.name: Security findings surfaced
items.summary: The reverse-engineer passes flagged cross-cutting security concerns (e.g. SSRF, sandbox flags) for human review that would otherwise stay buried in code.

categories.title: AI Data Products & Developer Experience
categories.role: Turn AI outputs into products people actually use, and lower the barrier for a whole team to work AI-first.

items.name: Nexus TA Dashboard
items.summary: A live GitHub Pages product surfacing test inventory, endpoint coverage, the nightly AI investigation, and this AI-platform overview — the consumable face of the whole system.

items.name: Morning Teams report
items.summary: A compact Adaptive Card posted to the team channel each morning with the AI summary, likely cause and top failures — the daily heartbeat.

items.name: Onboarding & enablement
items.summary: A `/welcome` orchestrator, the Cursor guide reference, a `/learn-backend-concept` tutor and task skills (e.g. add-REST-endpoint) get a new developer or agent productive quickly — the layer that makes the platform adoptable.
